mission.title: Install and Configure Elastic Cloud on Kubernetes (ECK)
mission.description: Elastic Cloud on Kubernetes (ECK) automates the deployment and management of Elastic Stack components on Kubernetes. It is ideal for users looking to run Elasticsearch, Kibana, and other Elastic products in a Kubernetes environment. Key trade-offs include the need for Kubernetes expertise and potential resource overhead.

steps.title: Add the Elastic Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the Elastic Helm repository to your Helm client, allowing you to install ECK from the official source.

```bash

helm repo add elastic https://helm.elastic.co

helm repo update

```

> This command adds the Elastic Helm repository.
> Updates the local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Install the ECK operator
steps.description:
This step installs the ECK operator in the 'elastic-system' namespace, which manages the lifecycle of Elastic Stack resources.

```bash

kubectl create namespace elastic-system

helm install eck-operator elastic/eck-operator --namespace elastic-system --version 2.14.0

```

> Creates a namespace for the ECK operator.
> Installs the ECK operator with a specific version.

steps.title: Verify the ECK operator installation
steps.description:
This step checks if the ECK operator is running correctly in the cluster.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n elastic-system

```

> Lists all pods in the elastic-system namespace to verify the operator is running.

steps.title: Deploy an Elasticsearch cluster
steps.description:
This step creates an Elasticsearch cluster using a custom resource definition (CRD).

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: elasticsearch.k8s.elastic.co/v1
kind: Elasticsearch
metadata:
  name: my-elasticsearch
  namespace: default
spec:
  version: 8.5.0
  nodeSets:
  - name: default
    count: 3
    config:
      node.store.allow_mmap: false
EOF

```

> Deploys a 3-node Elasticsearch cluster.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: elasticsearch.k8s.elastic.co/v1
kind: Elasticsearch
metadata:
  name: my-elasticsearch
  namespace: default
spec:
  version: 8.5.0
  nodeSets:
  - name: default
    count: 3
    config:
      node.store.allow_mmap: false
EOF

steps.title: Verify the Elasticsearch cluster
steps.description:
This step checks if the Elasticsearch cluster is running and ready.

```bash

kubectl get elasticsearch -n default

```

> Lists all Elasticsearch resources in the default namespace.

uninstall.title: Uninstall the ECK operator
uninstall.description:
This step removes the ECK operator and all associated resources. Be aware that this may lead to data loss if Elasticsearch clusters are not backed up.

```bash

helm uninstall eck-operator --namespace elastic-system

kubectl delete namespace elastic-system

```

> Uninstalls the ECK operator.
> Deletes the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade the ECK operator
upgrade.description:
This step involves backing up existing resources, upgrading the operator, and verifying the upgrade.

```bash

kubectl get elasticsearch -n default -o yaml > backup-elasticsearch.yaml

helm upgrade eck-operator elastic/eck-operator --namespace elastic-system --version 2.14.0

kubectl get pods -n elastic-system

```

> Backs up the current Elasticsearch configuration.
> Upgrades the ECK operator to the specified version.
> Verifies that the ECK operator pods are running after the upgrade.
commands: kubectl get elasticsearch -n default -o yaml > backup-elasticsearch.yaml

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to create Elasticsearch cluster'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur due to insufficient resources or misconfiguration.

**Fix:**
Check resource limits and ensure the cluster has enough CPU and memory allocated.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'ECK operator not found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the ECK operator is not installed or the namespace is incorrect.

**Fix:**
Ensure the ECK operator is installed in the correct namespace and check the Helm installation.

troubleshooting.title: Elasticsearch cluster is not ready
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This can happen if the pods are not starting due to resource constraints or configuration errors.

**Fix:**
Check the logs of the Elasticsearch pods using 'kubectl logs <pod-name>' for more details.

troubleshooting.title: Kibana is not accessible
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** Kibana may not be exposed correctly or the service is not running.

**Fix:**
Verify the Kibana service and check if it is correctly exposed using 'kubectl get svc -n default'.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for Elasticsearch 9.x, improved TLS certificate management.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from 2.13, ensure that your Elasticsearch configurations are compatible with 9.x.

resolution.summary: You should see the Elasticsearch cluster status as 'green' when running 'kubectl get elasticsearch -n default'.
codeSnippets:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: elasticsearch.k8s.elastic.co/v1
kind: Elasticsearch
metadata:
  name: my-elasticsearch
  namespace: default
spec:
  version: 8.5.0
  nodeSets:
  - name: default
    count: 3
    config:
      node.store.allow_mmap: false
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with sufficient resources.
